content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 20,
1993
Amelia Borcalli
City Counciliuember
City of Weed
550 Main Street
P.O..
Box 470
Weed,
CA
96094
Re:
Your Request for Formal Advice
Our File No.
A-93-292
Dear Ms. Borcalli:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities pursuant to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
1
QUESTIONS
1.
May you participate in the Weed City Council decision
concerning the Diliman Brewery project if Mr.
Diliman has been a
source of income to your hardware store in excess of $250 in the
12 months prior to the decision?
2.
Where you and another member of a five—member city
council are disqualified from participating in a decision
concerning the Diliman Brewery project due to conflicts of
interest,
may the exception for legally required participation be
invoked to permit you to requalify and participate in the
decision?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
You may not participate in the city council decision
relating to the Diliman Brewery project because Mr.
Dillman has
been a source of income to you of $250 or more within the
12
months prior to the decision.
2.
The exception for the legally required participation
exception is narrowly interpreted and only permits an otherwise
disqualified official to requalify and participate when there is
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—93-292
Page
2
no alternative source of decisionmaking.
Here, the decision has
been made by the city council and according to your letter, that
decision is not to move forward on the Dillman Brewery project at
this time.
Thus,
you may not requalify under this exception and
participate in the decision concerning the Diliman Brewery
project.
FACTS
According to your facts,
2 you are currently a Weed City
Councilmember and jointly own and operate, with your spouse,
a
hardware store in the jurisdiction.
The population of the city is
approximately 3,100.
There is one other hardware store in Weed.
You stated that the number of city residents who frequent your
hardware store easily exceeds
10 percent of the city’s population.
You do the bookkeeping for the business.
Your hardware store
offers open accounts to customers in addition to the over—the-
counter daily cash sales.
You estimate that many of the city’s
residents may have spent a total of $250 in your store in the
preceding 12 months.
The city council is considering the Dillman Brewery project.
In your letter,
you state that the project is a troublesome one
because it is opposed by those residents who live in the brewery
neighborhood.
However,
you believe the majority of the citizens
support your position concerning the project because it will bring
jobs and visitors to the city.
Two city councilmembers
(including yourself)
at present have
a conflict of interest which prevents them from participating in
any city decision concerning the Dillman Brewery project.
The
Weed City Council consists of five members and three
councilmembers constitute a quorum.
Three votes are needed to
secure the funding to continue the project,
including a tasting
room, gift shop,
and possibly a beer garden.
Yet,
one of the
three councilinembers without a conflict is opposed to the project
and his “no” vote will kill the project.
For this reason,
you
would like to vote on the project.
Total gross annual sales for your store in 1992 were $322,523
and in 1993 to date are $109,904.
Over the past two years,
Mr.
Dillman has made purchases aggregating $1,048 in 1993 and
$2,275 in 1992.
This means that in 1993,
Mr.
Dillman purchased
.007% or 7/10 of one percent of gross sales
($109,904)
and in
1992,
Mr.
Dillman purchased
.095% or almost one percent of gross
sales
($322,523).
2
Our letter responds to the information contained in your
letters of July 21,
1993 and August 9,
1993.
The Commission
generally provides advice within 21 working days of the request.
(Section 83114(b); Regulation 18329.)


Our File No. A-93-292
Page
3
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
A “public official” is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural
person who is a member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a
state or local government agency and would include Weed City
Couricilmembers.
Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Consequently,
any person or business that has made a payment
to your hardware store is a source of income to you.
Any person
or business which has been a source of income of $250 or more
within the 12 months prior to a decision under consideration by
the city council may be a disqualifying “financial interest” as
defined in Section 87103.
-
You stated that you and your spouse own a hardware store in
the jurisdiction.
Section 82030 provides that the income of an
individual also includes a pro—rata share of any income of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns,
directly,
indirectly or beneficially,
a 10-percent interest or
greater.
(Tracy Advice Letter,
No.
1-91-083.>
Thus, since
Mr.
Diliman has provided over $250 to your store,
you may not
participate in any decision which will have a foreseeable material
financial effect on Mr.
Diliman.
Once twelve months have elapsed
from the date of the $250 payment or any subsequent payments
aggregating $250 or more,
you will no longer have an economic
interest in Mr.
Dillman and may participate in decisions affecting
Mr.
Diliman.
Section 87103.5 provides a limited exception to the conflict
of—interest laws for income from retail sales of goods or
services.
Where the retail customers of the business constitute a
significant segment of the public generally,
and the amount of


Our File No.
A-93-292
Page
4
income received by the business from the customer is not
distinguishable from the amount of income received from the
business’ other retail customers, the exception provides that
income from the customer may be disregarded for conflict of
interest purposes of the Act.
The rationale behind the exception
is that if an individual customer is not distinguishable from
every other customer, the likelihood of favoritism is reduced.
Regulation 18703.5
(copy enclosed)
which interprets Section
87103.5 provides in pertinent part:
(a)
For purposes of Government Code Section
87103.5, the retail customers of a business entity
constitute a significant segment of the public
generally if either of the following is true:
(1)
The retail customers of the business
entity during the preceding 12 months are
sufficient in number to equal
10 percent or
more of the population or households of the
jurisdiction; or
(2)
The retail customers of the business
entity during the preceding 12 months number
at least ten thousand.
For purposes of this subdivision,
a customer
of a retail business entity is each a separate and
distinct purchaser of goods or services, whether an
individual, household,
business or other entity.
If records are not maintained by customer name,
a
good faith estimate shall be made to determine what
percentage of sales transactions represent multiple
transactions by repeat customers.
The total number
of sales transactions shall then be reduced by the
estimated percentage of repeat customers to yield
the number of customers for purposes of applying
this subdivision.
(b)
For purposes of Government Code Section
87103.5, the amount of income received by a
business entity from a retail customer is not
distinguishable from the amount of income received
from its other retail customers if the amount spent
by the customer in question during the preceding
12
months is less than one—tenth of
1 percent of the
gross sales revenues of the retail business entity
for the preceding fiscal year.
The threshold requirement of the exception is that the retail
customers of the official’s business over the preceding 12 months
constitute 10 percent or more of the population or households in
the jurisdiction or number at least ten thousand.
You stated that


Our File No. A-93-292
Page
5
more than 10 percent of the population of Weed frequent your
store.
However, before the exception can apply,
you also must
determine if the amount of income received by your store from a
retail customer is distinguishable from the amount of income
received from its other retail customers.
The regulation provides
that it is not distinguishable if the amount spent by the customer
in question during the preceding 12 months is less than one-tenth
of •one percent of the gross sales of the retail business entity
for the preceding fiscal year.
(Regulation 18703.5(b).)
When clients of your hardware store are directly involved in
a decision before the city council,
3 you must apply the above
standards to determine whether you must disqualify from
participating in the decision.
For example, if one of your
customers who is directly involved in a decision before the city
council has maintained an open charge account with your business
and has made purchases in excess of one—tenth of one percent of
your gross sales for the preceding fiscal year,
as appears to be
the case with Mr.
Dillman, pursuant to Regulation 18702.1(a)
you
must disqualify from participating in decisions involving this
customer.
This is so because the customer has provided an amount
of income to your business which is in excess of the income
received from a significant segment of your other retail
customers.
On the other hand, if a customer of your business is not
directly involved in a decision before the city council but will
be affected indirectly by the decision,
then you must apply the
standards set forth in either Regulation 18702.2
(if the customer
is a business)
or Regulation 18702.6 (if the customer is an
individual) to determine whether the financial effect of the
A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision
before an official’s agency when that person or entity, either
personally or by an agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim,
appeal,
or similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in,
or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official’s agency.
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance,
renewal,
approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit,
or other entitlement to,
or
contract with,
the subject person or business
entity.
Regulation 18702.1(b).


Our File No.
A-93—292
Page
6
decision will be material,
Copies of these regulations are
enclosed.
Public Generally Exception
Finally,
even if you have an economic interest that will be
materially affected by the decision,
you may still participate in
the decision if the effect on your economic interest is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
For the
“public generally” exception to apply, the decision must affect
your economic interest in substantially the same manner as it will
affect a significant segment of Weed.
(Regulation 18703.)
Here,
it appears unlikely that a decision concerning the Diliman Brewery
project will affect a significant segment of the population of
Weed in the same manner as the decision will affect your source of
income,
Mr.
Dillman.
Therefore,
we conclude that you are disqualified from
participating in city council decisions relating to the Diliman
Brewery project until
12 months have elapsed following the payment
of $250 or more from Mr.
Diliman to your business.
Participation
Please note that participation in governmental decisions has
been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act.
Thus,
where you are disqualified with respect to city council
decisions,
you are also prohibited from making contacts with,
appearing before,
or otherwise attempting to influence,
any
member, officer,
employee or consultant of your agency concerning
the governmental decision.
(Regulations 18700 and 18700.1.)
However,
you may communicate with the general public or the press.
(Regulation 18700.1(b) (5).)
Legally Required Participation
You ask whether you may vote on the Dillman Brewery project,
regardless of your conflict of interest,
so that the project may
move forward.
Section 87101 and Regulation 18701 provide a limited
exception where the public official’s participation is legally
required:
Section 87100 does not prevent any public
official from making or participating in the making
of a governmental decision to the extent his or her
participation is legally required for the action or
decision to be made.
Section 87101
(emphasis added).
(a)
A public official is not legally required
to make or to participate in the making of a


Our File No. A-93-292
Page
7
governmental decision within the meaning of
Government Code Section 87101 unless there exists
no alternative source of decision consistent with
the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing
the decision.
***
(c)
This regulation shall be construed
narrowly,
and shall:
(1)
Not be construed to permit an
official,
who is otherwise disqualified under
Government Code Section 87100, to vote to
break a tie.
(2)
Not be construed to allow a member
of any public agency,
who is otherwise
disqualified under Government Code Section
87100, to vote if a quorum can be convened of
other members of the agency who are not
disqualified under Government Code Section
87100, whether or not such other members are
actually present at the time of the
disqualification.
Emphasis added; Regulation 18701.
This exception has been narrowly interpreted to permit the
participation of the fewest financially interested persons
possible in any decision.
(In re Hudson
(1978)
4
FPPC Ops.
13.)
Consequently, where only a single member is needed to make a
quorum,
only one disqualified member is permitted to participate
in the decision.
This is because
“... the purposes of the Act are
best served by a rule which minimizes participation in government
decisions by officials with a conflict of interest.”
(In re
Hudson,
supra.)
You have asked whether these provisions might be interpreted
to permit your requalification.
Under the facts presented in your
letters, your situation does not come within this limited
exception.
The City of Weed has the necessary 3—member quorum;
this is not a situation where there is no alternative source of
decisionmaking.
On the contrary, it appears that the city council
has made a decision and that decision is not to move forward on
the Dillman Brewery project at this time.


Our File No.
A—93—292
Page
8
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59Ol.
Sincerely,
Wayne Ordos
Executive Director
Deanne Stone
Senior Commission Counsel
cc:
Deborah 3.
Salvestrin,
Weed Filing Officer
Enclosures
‘
copies of Commission regulations,
opinions and advice letters
may be obtained from the Commission at a cost of
1Oc per page.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1.
May you participate in the Weed City Council decision
concerning the Diliman Brewery project if Mr.
Diliman has been a
source of income to your hardware store in excess of $250 in the
12 months prior to the decision?
2.
Where you and another member of a five—member city
council are disqualified from participating in a decision
concerning the Diliman Brewery project due to conflicts of
interest,
may the exception for legally required participation be
invoked to permit you to requalify and participate in the
decision?

CONCLUSION: 1.
You may not participate in the city council decision
relating to the Diliman Brewery project because Mr.
Dillman has
been a source of income to you of $250 or more within the
12
months prior to the decision.
2.
The exception for the legally required participation
exception is narrowly interpreted and only permits an otherwise

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission August 20, 1993 Amelia Borcalli City Counciliuember City of Weed 550 Main Street P.O.. Box 470 Weed, CA 96094 Re: Your Request for Formal Advice Our File No. A-93-292 Dear Ms. Borcalli: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities pursuant to the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. 1 QUESTIONS 1. May you participate in the Weed City Council decision concerning the Diliman Brewery project if Mr. Diliman has been a source of income to your hardware store in excess of $250 in the 12 months prior to the decision? 2. Where you and another member of a five—member city council are disqualified from participating in a decision concerning the Diliman Brewery project due to conflicts of interest, may the exception for legally required participation be invoked to permit you to requalify and participate in the decision? CONCLUSIONS 1. You may not participate in the city council decision relating to the Diliman Brewery project because Mr. Dillman has been a source of income to you of $250 or more within the 12 months prior to the decision. 2. The exception for the legally required participation exception is narrowly interpreted and only permits an otherwise disqualified official to requalify and participate when there is 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et g. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A—93-292 Page 2 no alternative source of decisionmaking. Here, the decision has been made by the city council and according to your letter, that decision is not to move forward on the Dillman Brewery project at this time. Thus, you may not requalify under this exception and participate in the decision concerning the Diliman Brewery project. FACTS According to your facts, 2 you are currently a Weed City Councilmember and jointly own and operate, with your spouse, a hardware store in the jurisdiction. The population of the city is approximately 3,100. There is one other hardware store in Weed. You stated that the number of city residents who frequent your hardware store easily exceeds 10 percent of the city’s population. You do the bookkeeping for the business. Your hardware store offers open accounts to customers in addition to the over—the- counter daily cash sales. You estimate that many of the city’s residents may have spent a total of $250 in your store in the preceding 12 months. The city council is considering the Dillman Brewery project. In your letter, you state that the project is a troublesome one because it is opposed by those residents who live in the brewery neighborhood. However, you believe the majority of the citizens support your position concerning the project because it will bring jobs and
